name: Web Push Notifications
description: Native OS-level push notifications, delivered even with the app closed — Service Worker + VAPID end to end.
longDescription: A full Web Push flow built the way it actually works in production: the browser generates a subscription signed with the VAPID public key, the backend signs every send with the private key through web-push, and delivery happens via the Service Worker's push event — which is why the notification arrives even with the tab, or the whole browser, closed. The demo walks through the three real steps of the flow (permission → device registration → test send) and, being a demo, keeps subscriptions in server memory instead of a database.
tags: Push API, Notifications API, Service Worker, VAPID, Web Push Protocol
architecture: Push API + Notifications API on top of a Service Worker, with VAPID signing (public key on the client, full key pair on the server via web-push) and asynchronous delivery through the Service Worker's push event
technologies: Push API, Notifications API, Service Worker, web-push (VAPID), Next.js API Routes
concepts: Push subscription, background delivery via Service Worker, browser permission lifecycle, VAPID signing, Web Push Protocol
desc1: Push Notifications are notifications sent by the application directly to the user's device, even when the site or PWA isn't open at that moment.
desc2: They work similarly to regular app notifications, showing up on the phone or computer screen to report events, updates, or important alerts.
canDoIntro: In practice, they can be used to:
pwaNote: In PWAs, Push Notifications help bring the web experience even closer to a native app experience.
whyItWorksDesc: This works because delivery doesn't depend on the application being open: the browser keeps a low-level connection to the push service, which wakes the Service Worker specifically for the push event and displays the notification — even with the tab, or the entire browser, closed.
valueNote: The main advantage is keeping the user engaged even outside the application, enabling real-time communication without needing to install a traditional app from a store.
howToUseIntro: The notification flow happens in 3 steps:
step1: 1. Allow the application to use device notifications.
step2: 2. Register this device to receive notifications.
permissionIntro: First, allow the application to use your browser's notification center:
subscribeIntro: Then, register this device so it can receive push notifications:
sendIntro: Finally, send a test notification to confirm everything is working correctly:
demoWarning: Heads up: this demo keeps subscriptions in server memory for testing purposes only — they're lost on every new deploy and aren't used for anything else.
unsupportedMessage: Push Notifications aren't supported in this browser. Use an up-to-date desktop or mobile browser with Service Worker and Push API support.
permissionDeniedMessage: Notification permission denied. To use this demo, enable notifications for this site in your browser settings and reload the page.
subscribeErrorMessage: Couldn't register this device for notifications. Please try again.
sendErrorMessage: Couldn't send the test notification. Please try again.
method1: Notification.requestPermission() — asks the user for permission to display notifications
method2: navigator.serviceWorker.ready — waits for the active Service Worker before registering the subscription
method3: registration.pushManager.subscribe() — creates the push subscription authenticated with the VAPID public key
method4: fetch('/api/push/subscribe') — sends the subscription to the backend to be stored
method5: webpush.sendNotification() (server) — signs and sends the notification payload using the VAPID key pair
method6: self.addEventListener('push', ...) (Service Worker) — receives the push event in the background and prepares the notification
